appeal_letter:
Respectfully submitted as an appeal of the denial of coverage for Adalimumab (Humira) administered on 04/30/2026.

The denial states that coverage was refused because prior authorization was not obtained before the medication was dispensed. The letter explains that specialty biologic medications require prior authorization from Aetna's Pharmacy Benefit Manager to confirm clinical appropriateness and that step-therapy protocols have been met, and that, because the provider did not submit a prior authorization request prior to the date of service, the member is responsible for the full cost.

I ask the plan to confront that stated ground directly against the documented facts of this case. The criterion the denial relies on is the requirement that the medication be clinically appropriate and that step-therapy protocols be satisfied. Those clinical purposes are met here. I was previously prescribed and stabilized on Humira under a prior insurance plan. I then changed jobs and changed insurance to Aetna, and my physician continued the existing prescription through the transition period specifically to prevent a severe joint flare-up. The lapse the denial identifies was procedural: at the moment of the transition, a fresh prior authorization had not yet been filed with the new plan. It was not a clinical lapse. On the individualized facts, the drug was clinically appropriate and reflected continuation of an established, effective therapy rather than a new or unjustified start.

Where the denial treats the absence of a pre-service prior authorization as dispositive, I ask the plan to weigh the specific circumstances and grant the medication on review. The interruption arose from the timing of an insurance change during ongoing treatment, and continuing the established therapy was directed by my physician to avoid a severe flare. I ask that the prior authorization be considered and approved on these facts, and that coverage of the 04/30/2026 administration be reconsidered accordingly.

I also note that the denial letter does not state an appeal deadline, does not describe the process for submitting an appeal, and does not state the member's right to a full and fair review. I ask the plan to confirm these items in writing and to provide a complete and fair review of this matter.

This letter is a request for review and does not waive any rights. I am prepared to supply additional documentation on request.

Not legal or medical advice. Draft assistance only.

Respectfully,
Member
missing_evidence_checklist: Provider records documenting prior stability on Humira under the previous insurance plan
Documentation of the dates of the job change and the Aetna coverage effective date
A letter of medical necessity from the prescribing physician explaining the continuity-of-care decision and the risk of a severe joint flare
Any prior authorization or step-therapy approval issued under the previous plan
The plan documents describing the prior authorization requirement and any continuity-of-care or transition-of-care provisions
The pharmacy dispensing record and claim details for the 04/30/2026 administration
